pageIntro.definition: Push one cart with a timed force pulse and watch momentum, impulse, and force-time area stay tied to the same motion, readouts, and graphs.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Momentum is mass times velocity, and impulse is signed force-time area; matching that area matches the momentum change even when the pulse shape changes.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: Impulse turns a force history into a precise momentum change, so you can predict how hard, how long, and in what direction a push will change motion.

paragraphs: Momentum tells you how much motion an object is carrying: in one dimension it is $p = mv$, so a heavier cart or a faster cart has more momentum. The sign matters too, because momentum points with the velocity.

Impulse is what a force does over a stretch of time. In this module one bounded force pulse pushes a cart on a fixed track, so you can see the pulse shape, the accumulated impulse, and the momentum graph move together without inventing a separate subsystem for collisions.

keyIdeas: Momentum is mass times velocity, so the same speed change does not mean the same momentum change for every mass.
Impulse is the signed force-time area. A short large force and a smaller longer force can produce the same total impulse if the areas match.
For the same cart, impulse changes momentum directly through $J = \Delta p$, which is why the momentum graph and the accumulated-impulse graph should agree.

commonMisconception.myth: A bigger force always means a bigger momentum change, even if it acts for less time.
correction: Momentum change depends on the total impulse, not force alone. A large force over a very short interval can match the effect of a smaller force acting longer.
Mass changes how much the velocity shifts for a given impulse, but it does not change the fact that the momentum change itself is set by $J = F\Delta t$.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Solve the pulse you are actually watching. Time-based examples follow the current inspected time unless you freeze them, and the pulse example keeps the full force-duration pair tied to the real controls.

items.title: Current momentum
items.prompt: At $t = {{timeValue}}\,\mathrm{s}$, what is the cart's momentum?

steps.label: 1. Identify the relation
steps.template: Use $p = mv$ for the cart at the currently inspected moment.

applyAction.label: Apply short hard push
applyAction.observationHint: Watch the momentum graph, the cart speed arrow, and the stage bars together. They should all be describing the same moment.

items.title: Impulse from the pulse
items.prompt: For the current force pulse, what impulse does the cart receive and what final momentum should that predict?

steps.label: 1. Start from impulse-momentum
steps.template: Use $J = F\Delta t$ and $p_f = p_i + J$ for this constant pulse.

applyAction.label: Apply long gentle push
applyAction.observationHint: Compare the wider force pulse with the same final jump in the momentum and impulse graphs.

miniChallenge.prompt: Can you choose a new force-duration pair that leaves the final momentum unchanged?
miniChallenge.prediction: Try making the pulse shorter while raising the force, or longer while lowering the force. Predict which pairs should keep the same final momentum.
miniChallenge.answer: Any pair with the same signed area under the force-time graph gives the same momentum change for the same cart.
miniChallenge.explanation: This is the bounded version of the impulse idea. If $F\Delta t$ stays the same, then $J$ stays the same, so the momentum change stays the same even though the pulse shape looks different.

equations.label: Momentum
equations.meaning: Momentum combines how much mass is moving with how fast it moves.

equations.label: Impulse for a constant pulse
equations.meaning: For a constant force, impulse is the force-time area.

equations.label: Impulse-momentum theorem
equations.meaning: Impulse changes momentum directly, which is why the accumulated impulse and momentum shift should match.

variableLinks.label: Mass
variableLinks.description: Heavier carts need more impulse for the same velocity change, even though the momentum change still follows the same force-time area.

variableLinks.label: Initial velocity
variableLinks.description: Sets the starting direction and the starting momentum before the pulse begins.

variableLinks.label: Force
variableLinks.description: Sets the height and sign of the pulse, so it controls how quickly the cart's momentum changes while the pulse is on.

variableLinks.label: Pulse duration
variableLinks.description: Sets how long the force stays on, so it widens or narrows the force-time area and changes the total impulse.

controls.label: Mass
controls.description: Change how much mass is moving without changing the force pulse.

controls.label: Initial velocity
controls.description: Set the cart's starting motion before the pulse begins.

controls.label: Force
controls.description: Positive force pushes right. Negative force pushes left.

controls.label: Pulse duration
controls.description: Set how long the force stays on during the fixed pulse window.

presets.label: Long gentle push
presets.description: A lower, wider pulse with the same $J = 1.2\,\mathrm{N\,s}$.

presets.label: Heavy cart
presets.description: Keep the same pulse but triple the mass.

presets.label: Braking pulse
presets.description: A leftward pulse reduces the cart's rightward momentum.

overlays.label: Force arrow
overlays.shortDescription: Shows the direction and size of the applied force on the cart.
whatToNotice: The arrow only matters during the pulse window. Before and after that, the cart coasts.
Reversing the arrow reverses the sign of the impulse and the momentum change.
overlays.whyItMatters: Impulse is signed, so direction matters just as much as size.

overlays.label: Pulse window
overlays.shortDescription: Shades the time interval where the force is on.
whatToNotice: The shaded interval is time, not distance.
Widening the pulse can keep the same impulse even when the force gets smaller.
overlays.whyItMatters: The force-time area is what sets impulse.

overlays.label: Momentum bars
overlays.shortDescription: Compares the initial, current, and final momentum on one centered scale.
whatToNotice: The middle bar moves while the pulse is active, then freezes when the force returns to zero.
The gap from $p_i$ to $p_f$ matches the total impulse.
overlays.whyItMatters: The bars make $J = \Delta p$ visible without leaving the same stage.

graphs.label: Force vs time
graphs.description: Shows the height, sign, and width of the force pulse.

graphs.label: Momentum vs time
graphs.description: Shows momentum staying flat before and after the pulse and changing only while the force acts.

graphs.label: Impulse and change in momentum
graphs.description: The accumulated impulse and the change in momentum should sit on top of each other for the same cart.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompt as a short guide while you change the pulse. The best prompt should point at something the stage and the graph can both honestly show right now.

items.text: Notice that the pulse area on the force graph is the same quantity that appears as the momentum change.
items.tryThis: Watch the force pulse and the accumulated-impulse graph together. The momentum graph should shift by the same signed amount.
items.whyItMatters: This is the impulse-momentum theorem in one bounded visual loop.

items.text: Notice that momentum only changes while the force is actually on.
items.tryThis: Pause before the pulse, during it, and after it. The momentum graph should be flat, sloped, then flat again.
items.whyItMatters: It separates the effect of a force from motion that simply continues afterward.

items.text: Notice that a lower wider pulse can end at the same final momentum as a taller narrower pulse.
items.tryThis: Compare the short hard push with the long gentle push. The force graph should look different even when the final momentum matches.
items.whyItMatters: It breaks the lazy rule that only force size matters.

items.text: Notice that changing pulse duration widens the force-time area even when the force height stays fixed.
items.tryThis: Lengthen the pulse without changing the force. The force graph gets wider and the final momentum shifts farther.
items.whyItMatters: Impulse is not about force alone. Time matters just as directly.

items.text: Notice that mass changes the velocity response more than the momentum change for the same impulse.
items.tryThis: Keep the same pulse and compare a light cart with a heavy cart. The impulse graph should match even though the speed change on the stage is smaller for the heavy cart.
items.whyItMatters: It separates $\Delta p$ from $\Delta v$, which students often collapse into one idea.

items.text: Notice that the impulse graph should stop climbing exactly when the force pulse ends.
items.tryThis: Scrub the graph near the end of the pulse and compare the time cursor with the shaded pulse window.
items.whyItMatters: It shows that accumulated impulse is a running total, not a permanent force.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict the pulse first, then apply the change and compare the cart, the force graph, and the momentum shift.

items.prompt: If you switch from the short hard push to the long gentle push on the same $1\,\mathrm{kg}$ cart, what should stay the same?

choices.label: The final momentum change stays the same, even though the force graph gets lower and wider.
The final momentum must get smaller because the peak force is smaller.
Nothing changes because force and time always cancel out completely.

items.explanation: Both presets deliver the same signed impulse, so they give the same momentum change to the same cart even though the pulse shapes differ.
items.observationHint: Compare the end of the momentum graph and the final momentum bars after the long gentle pulse is applied.

items.prompt: If mass increases while the same short hard pulse is applied, what should the stage and graphs show?
items.scenarioLabel: Heavy cart with the same pulse

choices.label: The momentum change stays the same, but the cart's velocity changes less.
The impulse gets smaller automatically because the cart is heavier.
The force graph must get taller to keep the momentum graph honest.

items.explanation: The force-time area is unchanged, so the impulse and momentum change stay the same. The heavier cart simply converts that same $\Delta p$ into a smaller $\Delta v$.
items.observationHint: Watch the momentum graph move by the same amount while the cart speed and spacing on the track change less dramatically.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Use the force pulse, the cart motion, and the graphs together. Each question checks whether you can reason with momentum and impulse, not just recite a definition.

questions.prompt: A $3\,\mathrm{N}$ force for $0.4\,\mathrm{s}$ and a $1.5\,\mathrm{N}$ force for $0.8\,\mathrm{s}$ act on the same cart in the same direction. Which statement is correct?

choices.label: They can produce the same momentum change because the force-time areas are equal.
The $3\,\mathrm{N}$ force must produce the larger momentum change because it is taller on the graph.
The $1.5\,\mathrm{N}$ force must produce zero momentum change because it is gentler.

questions.explanation: Impulse depends on the signed area under the force-time graph. If those areas match, then the momentum changes match for the same cart.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Height alone is not enough. The shorter duration of the $3\,\mathrm{N}$ pulse can balance the larger peak force.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: A gentle force can still change momentum if it acts long enough to build nonzero impulse.

showMeAction.label: Show the long gentle case
showMeAction.observationHint: The force pulse is lower and wider, but the final momentum bar lands in the same place as the short hard push.

questions.prompt: If the same force pulse acts on a heavier cart, what changes most directly?

choices.label: The same impulse gives the same momentum change, but a smaller velocity change.
The impulse disappears because heavy carts resist force completely.
The momentum change must get larger because heavier carts always gain more motion.

questions.explanation: Impulse fixes $\Delta p$. Since $p = mv$, a larger mass means the same momentum change corresponds to a smaller change in velocity.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: A heavier cart can still receive the same impulse. The effect shows up as a smaller speed shift, not zero momentum change.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Heavier carts can have more momentum at the same velocity, but the momentum change from a fixed pulse is still set by $J$.

showMeAction.label: Show the heavy cart
showMeAction.observationHint: The momentum graph still climbs by the same amount, but the cart speed after the pulse is less dramatic.

questions.prompt: What should the momentum and accumulated-impulse graphs do after the force pulse has ended?

choices.label: Both should become flat because no additional impulse is being added.
Momentum should keep sloping forever because the cart is still moving.
The impulse graph should reset to zero as soon as the force turns off.

questions.explanation: Once the force returns to zero, the running impulse stops changing, so the momentum stops changing too. Motion can continue even while both graphs are flat.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Motion continuing is not the same as momentum continuing to change. Without force, the existing momentum simply persists.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Accumulated impulse is a running total. It stays at the delivered value after the pulse rather than resetting itself.

showMeAction.label: Show the pulse ending
showMeAction.observationHint: Pause after the pulse and compare the flat tail of the impulse graph with the flat tail of the momentum graph.

questions.prompt: Which explanation best corrects the claim that a larger force must always create a larger momentum change?

choices.label: You must include the time interval too, because impulse depends on the full force-time area.
Momentum change depends only on the cart's starting speed, not on force.
Momentum change depends only on mass because force is temporary.

questions.explanation: Force height matters, but it is only part of the area. A smaller force can still create the same or larger momentum change if it acts longer.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Starting speed sets the initial momentum, but the change in momentum comes from the applied impulse.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Mass affects how velocity responds, but impulse still depends on force and time together.

showMeAction.label: Show the braking pulse
showMeAction.observationHint: The negative pulse changes momentum in the opposite direction, which shows that sign and duration matter too.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a cart moving on a fixed horizontal track while a single force pulse turns on during a highlighted time window. The cart label shows mass, a horizontal arrow shows velocity, and overlays can emphasize the force direction, the pulse window, and centered bars for initial, current, and final momentum.
Changing mass, initial velocity, force, or pulse duration immediately updates the cart motion, the pulse timing, the readouts, and the linked graphs without changing the underlying scale.
The force graph shows a rectangular pulse that can point positive or negative. The momentum graph is flat before and after the pulse and changes only while the force acts.
The impulse graph and the change-in-momentum graph should overlap because they represent the same signed quantity for the same cart.

seo.description: Interactive momentum and impulse lesson with a timed force pulse, linked momentum graphs, compare mode, prediction prompts, and conceptual quick tests.

v2.intuition: Watch one cart through one force pulse so momentum change and force-time area stay tied to the same moving object.
v2.whyItMatters: Impulse is the durable bridge from a force history to a momentum change, so this bench needs to feel like one cause-and-effect chain instead of two formulas.

guidedSteps.title: Read one force pulse
guidedSteps.goal: Treat the force pulse as an area that changes the cart momentum.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the short-hard-push preset, keep the force graph visible, and scrub one full pulse.
guidedSteps.notice: The force-time graph builds a finite area while the momentum readout changes over the same interval.
guidedSteps.explain: Impulse is the accumulated force over time, so the area under the force graph is the same quantity that changes momentum.

guidedSteps.title: Compare two equal impulses
guidedSteps.goal: See why different force shapes can still land the same momentum change.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch between the short-hard-push and long-gentle-push presets and compare the final momentum each pulse leaves behind.
guidedSteps.notice: One pulse is taller and shorter, the other is lower and wider, but the final momentum change can match.
guidedSteps.explain: The details of force versus time can differ while the total area stays the same, so equal impulse gives equal momentum change.

guidedSteps.title: Change mass, not impulse
guidedSteps.goal: Separate momentum change from speed change by making the cart heavier.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply the heavy-cart preset and compare the resulting speed change with the earlier pulse cases.
guidedSteps.notice: The same kind of impulse can produce a smaller speed change when the cart mass is larger.
guidedSteps.explain: Impulse changes momentum directly. The resulting speed change also depends on mass because p = mv.

guidedSteps.title: Build a matching impulse
guidedSteps.goal: Use force and duration together to land a target momentum change instead of chasing force height alone.
guidedSteps.doThis: Open the challenge and make a lower, longer pulse that still lands near the same final momentum as the short hard push.
guidedSteps.notice: The force graph looks different, but the impulse readout and final momentum can still end in the same band.
guidedSteps.explain: This is the impulse-momentum theorem as a design rule: keep the signed area fixed and the momentum change stays fixed for the same starting cart.

learned: Momentum combines mass, speed, and direction.
Impulse is the signed area under the force-time graph.
Equal impulse gives the same momentum change even when the force pulse has a different shape.
A heavier cart can get the same momentum change with a smaller change in speed.
wrapUp.misconception: A bigger force automatically means a bigger final momentum change, even if the pulse is much shorter.
wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Build a lower, longer pulse and a taller, shorter pulse that land in the same final-momentum band, then try a braking pulse and explain the sign change.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the force and duration controls as an area-matching tool: the graph shape may change, but the final momentum should prove whether the impulse matched.

items.title: Match momentum with a gentler pulse
items.prompt: Starting from the short hard push, build a lower, longer rightward pulse that still delivers nearly the same impulse and final momentum.
items.successMessage: You matched the impulse without copying the force height. The lower, longer pulse lands in the same final-momentum band because the signed force-time area stayed nearly the same.

setup.note: Use the force and pulse-duration sliders together. The target is not a tall force; it is matching the final momentum.

hints: The baseline impulse is about $1.2\,\mathrm{N\,s}$, so lowering force means you need a longer pulse.
A force near $1.5\,\mathrm{N}$ and a duration near $0.8\,\mathrm{s}$ should keep the area close.
Check the final momentum bar, not just the peak height of the force graph.

targets.label: Stretch the pulse duration to roughly $0.8\,\mathrm{s}$.

items.title: Brake with negative impulse
items.prompt: Make a right-moving cart receive a leftward pulse, then explain how the negative force-time area reduces the final momentum.
items.successMessage: You used impulse as a signed quantity: the leftward force arrow creates negative area, so the cart keeps moving only if its starting momentum is larger than the braking impulse.

setup.note: Keep the force graph and momentum bars visible so the sign of the impulse is not hidden.

hints: Negative force makes the impulse graph step downward during the pulse.
The final momentum should be lower than the initial momentum even though the cart began by moving right.
